description: Diagram illustrating how the same LLM prompt can produce different outputs across multiple runs, showing the stochastic nature of LLM responses and how silent failures occur when prompts are accidentally modified
teaching_value: Helps students visualize why LLM non-determinism is a production engineering problem — unlike code errors that throw exceptions, prompt changes fail silently with varied outputs
when_to_show: When introducing why LLMs are difficult to deploy reliably in production; use before discussing temperature=0 as a mitigation strategy
suggested_caption: Same prompt, different outputs: LLMs produce inconsistent results by default. (Chip Huyen)

description: Diagram illustrating the analogy of a teacher giving consistent scores only in one specific room — visualizing how setting temperature=0 achieves consistency but doesn't fully solve trust or reliability issues in LLM outputs
teaching_value: Concretizes the limitation of temperature=0 as a consistency fix: it constrains variance but doesn't address deeper reliability concerns, helping students understand why consistency alone isn't sufficient for production trust
when_to_show: After explaining temperature=0 as a fix for stochasticity; use to prompt discussion about what 'reliable' really means beyond just determinism
suggested_caption: Temperature=0 enforces consistency but doesn't guarantee trustworthy LLM outputs. (Chip Huyen)

description: Comparison diagram contrasting prompting versus finetuning approaches for adapting LLMs, likely showing trade-offs in cost, latency, flexibility, and model size across the two paradigms
teaching_value: Gives students a side-by-side framework for deciding when to use prompting vs. finetuning, grounding the decision in practical engineering constraints like latency and model scale
when_to_show: When introducing the prompting vs. finetuning decision; use as a decision-making framework before diving into data requirements for each approach
suggested_caption: Prompting vs. finetuning: trade-offs in cost, latency, and flexibility. (Chip Huyen)

alt: Prompting vs. finetuning: number of examples needed
description: Chart showing the relationship between number of training examples and the recommended approach (prompting vs. finetuning), illustrating that prompting works with few examples while finetuning requires hundreds or more
teaching_value: Gives students a concrete, data-driven heuristic for choosing between prompting and finetuning based on available labeled data, making an abstract decision tangible
when_to_show: When students ask 'how much data do I need to finetune?' — use this to anchor the answer and transition into discussing prompt tuning as a middle ground
suggested_caption: Number of examples needed: prompting vs. finetuning decision boundary. (Chip Huyen)

description: Diagram illustrating prompt tuning: instead of modifying the text of a prompt, the embedding of the prompt is programmatically optimized, showing performance comparison with prompt engineering and full model tuning on T5
teaching_value: Introduces a nuanced technique between prompting and finetuning, helping students understand that model adaptation can happen at the embedding level without changing model weights or prompt text
when_to_show: After covering prompting and finetuning; introduce as a bridge technique when discussing parameter-efficient adaptation methods
suggested_caption: Prompt tuning optimizes prompt embeddings, bridging prompting and finetuning. (Chip Huyen)

description: Diagram illustrating the Alpaca distillation pipeline: 52k instructions fed into text-davinci-003 (175B parameters) to generate outputs, which are then used to finetune LLaMA-7B, producing a small model that mimics a large one
teaching_value: Makes the distillation concept concrete with real model names, parameter counts, and costs — students can see exactly how a $600 pipeline produces a capable small model from a large teacher model
when_to_show: When teaching distillation or cost-efficient LLM deployment; use to show students that frontier model capabilities can be approximated cheaply
suggested_caption: Stanford Alpaca: distilling text-davinci-003 into LLaMA-7B for under $600. (Chip Huyen)

description: Diagram showing four LLM agent control flow patterns: sequential (tasks in order), parallel (tasks simultaneously), if-statement (conditional branching), and for-loop (iterative execution), with LLM-determined conditions
teaching_value: Transfers familiar software engineering control flow concepts into the LLM agent context, helping students understand that agents are programmable pipelines where branching logic can itself be determined by LLM outputs
when_to_show: When introducing LLM agent architectures; use to show students how traditional programming constructs map onto prompt-driven orchestration
suggested_caption: LLM agent control flows: sequential, parallel, if-statement, and for-loop patterns. (Chip Huyen)

description: Screenshot of Amazon search results for 'things you need for camping in oregon in november', showing keyword-based results that return generic camping product listings rather than a curated, context-aware list of needed items.
teaching_value: Illustrates the limitation of traditional keyword-based search engines — they match literal terms rather than understanding user intent or context, motivating the need for LLM-enhanced search.
when_to_show: Show this before introducing LLM-powered search to establish the problem: keyword search fails to interpret contextual, open-ended queries.
suggested_caption: Traditional keyword search fails contextual queries like camping needs. (Huyenchip.com)

description: Diagram showing a multi-step LLM pipeline for intelligent search: breaking down a natural language query (e.g., 'things you need for camping in Oregon in November') into structured steps — likely including query understanding, item generation, and product retrieval — to return a curated, context-aware product list.
teaching_value: Demonstrates how LLMs can be chained in a pipeline to transform an open-ended natural language query into actionable, structured search results, teaching the concept of LLM application decomposition into steps.
when_to_show: Show after presenting the keyword-search limitation (Image 1) to illustrate how an LLM-powered pipeline solves the problem of intent-aware search.
suggested_caption: LLM pipeline decomposes a natural language query into structured search steps. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A follow-up or alternative diagram of the LLM-based search pipeline, likely showing a refined or extended version of the steps for converting a contextual user query into product recommendations, possibly including additional stages such as ranking, filtering, or LLM-generated item lists fed into a product database.
teaching_value: Reinforces the multi-step LLM application architecture for search, potentially showing additional pipeline stages or a variant approach, helping students understand how LLM components can be composed and extended for real-world e-commerce use cases.
when_to_show: Show alongside or immediately after Image 2 to provide a complementary or extended view of the LLM search pipeline, useful when discussing pipeline variants or additional steps like ranking and filtering.
suggested_caption: Extended LLM search pipeline for context-aware product recommendations. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A step-by-step dialogue showing the Self-Ask prompting method, where the LLM decomposes a complex question into follow-up sub-questions, issues external search queries for each, retrieves answers, and synthesizes a final answer.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how Self-Ask breaks multi-hop reasoning into explicit intermediate steps with external search, helping students understand how LLMs can be guided to decompose problems rather than answer in one shot.
when_to_show: Show when introducing retrieval-augmented or decomposition-based prompting strategies, especially to contrast with standard single-pass prompting.
suggested_caption: Self-Ask: LLM decomposes questions and queries external search. (Press et al. 2022)

description: A diagram comparing four reasoning strategies side by side: Input→Output (standard), Chain-of-Thought, Self-Consistency with CoT, and Tree of Thoughts — showing how ToT explores a branching tree of intermediate reasoning steps with evaluation and backtracking.
teaching_value: Visually contrasts the progression from simple prompting to tree-structured search over reasoning paths, helping students understand why ToT outperforms linear CoT by allowing exploration and pruning of thought branches.
when_to_show: Show when teaching advanced reasoning prompting methods, particularly after covering CoT and self-consistency, to illustrate the conceptual leap to tree-based exploration.
suggested_caption: Tree of Thoughts explores branching reasoning paths vs. linear CoT. (Yao et al. 2022)

description: A side-by-side comparison of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Program of Thoughts (PoT) prompting on a math word problem, showing CoT producing natural language reasoning steps while PoT generates Python code that is executed by an interpreter to compute the answer.
teaching_value: Clearly demonstrates the key difference between verbal reasoning (CoT) and code-based reasoning (PoT), helping students understand how offloading computation to a Python interpreter improves accuracy on arithmetic-heavy tasks.
when_to_show: Show when introducing programming-language-based prompting methods (PAL/PoT), especially to contrast with CoT and motivate why symbolic execution is more reliable for math.
suggested_caption: PoT generates executable code instead of verbal reasoning steps. (Chen et al. 2022)

description: A formatted text example showing the TALM API call structure, with special tokens |tool-call, tool input, |result, and |output demarcating where the LLM generates a tool request, where the external API result is inserted, and where the final answer is produced.
teaching_value: Makes the abstract concept of tool-augmented LMs concrete by showing the exact token-level format used to interleave LLM generation with external API calls, helping students understand how tool use is implemented mechanically.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how LLMs interface with external tools at the token level, before or alongside Toolformer to compare approaches.
suggested_caption: TALM uses special tokens to structure tool calls and results. (Parisi et al. 2022)

description: A performance graph showing model accuracy across multiple self-play iterations in TALM, demonstrating that iterative bootstrapping of tool-use training examples progressively improves the model's performance on downstream tasks.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that the self-play iteration strategy in TALM is effective, helping students connect the theoretical mechanism of iterative dataset expansion to measurable performance gains.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the TALM self-play mechanism to provide quantitative justification for why iterative tool-use training works.
suggested_caption: TALM self-play iterations progressively improve model performance. (Parisi et al. 2022)

description: A pipeline diagram of Toolformer's training process: starting from raw text, generating candidate API call annotations via in-context learning, executing the API calls, filtering annotations by whether they reduce loss on future tokens, and fine-tuning the LM on the filtered dataset.
teaching_value: Illustrates the full self-supervised pipeline for teaching an LLM to use tools without human-labeled data, helping students understand how Toolformer bootstraps tool-use capability from a few demonstrations.
when_to_show: Show when teaching Toolformer as a case study in self-supervised tool augmentation, after introducing TALM to highlight the shared self-play philosophy and differences in implementation.
suggested_caption: Toolformer pipeline: annotate, execute, filter, and fine-tune on API calls. (Schick et al. 2023)

description: A concrete text example showing how Toolformer annotates a sentence with API calls — embedding calculator, Wikipedia search, and calendar tool calls inline within natural text, with results inserted, demonstrating the annotation format used to create training data.
teaching_value: Shows students exactly how multiple tool types (calculator, search, calendar) are embedded in natural text as annotations, making the abstract data-creation process tangible and clarifying how diverse tools are unified under one format.
when_to_show: Show alongside the Toolformer pipeline diagram to ground the abstract training steps in a concrete annotated example, especially when discussing how different tool types are handled uniformly.
suggested_caption: Toolformer annotates text with inline API calls for calculator, search, and calendar. (Schick et al. 2023)

description: A high-level architecture diagram of an LLM-powered autonomous agent system, showing the LLM as the central brain connected to components: Planning (with sub-components like reflection and decomposition), Memory (sensory, short-term, long-term), and Tool Use (external APIs, databases, etc.).
teaching_value: Gives students a mental map of the entire agent system before diving into individual components, helping them understand how planning, memory, and tool use interconnect around the LLM core.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on LLM agents to establish the overall system structure before explaining each subsystem in detail.
suggested_caption: LLM agent architecture: planning, memory, and tool use components. (Weng, 2023)

description: Side-by-side comparison of ReAct reasoning trajectories for knowledge-intensive tasks (HotpotQA, FEVER) and decision-making tasks (AlfWorld, WebShop), showing interleaved Thought, Action, and Observation steps versus Act-only baselines.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how ReAct interleaves reasoning traces with actions, making the abstract concept of 'reasoning + acting' tangible through real task examples and showing why the Thought step matters.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the ReAct framework, specifically to contrast the Thought-Action-Observation loop against an Act-only approach and explain why reasoning traces improve performance.
suggested_caption: ReAct reasoning trajectories across knowledge and decision-making tasks. (Yao et al., 2023)

description: A flowchart illustrating the Reflexion framework, showing the cycle between Actor (LLM), Evaluator (reward/heuristic), Self-Reflection module, and Memory buffer, with arrows indicating how failed trajectories feed into self-reflection and updated memory for the next trial.
teaching_value: Visualizes the feedback loop that enables iterative self-improvement in agents, clarifying how dynamic memory and self-reflection work together to correct mistakes across trials.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how agents can self-correct, particularly after introducing the concept of trial-and-error in autonomous agents and before discussing the heuristic function details.
suggested_caption: Reflexion's self-reflection loop: actor, evaluator, and memory buffer. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)

description: Bar charts comparing Reflexion against baselines on AlfWorld and HotpotQA benchmarks, broken down by failure type (hallucination vs. inefficient planning), showing performance across multiple trials.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence for Reflexion's effectiveness and quantifies the relative frequency of hallucination versus inefficient planning as failure modes, grounding theoretical concepts in measurable outcomes.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the Reflexion framework to validate its claims with data, and use the hallucination vs. inefficient planning breakdown to deepen discussion of agent failure modes.
suggested_caption: Reflexion outperforms baselines; hallucination dominates over inefficient planning. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)

description: A diagram showing how Chain of Hindsight (CoH) fine-tuning works: a sequence of model outputs annotated with feedback labels (Bad, Good, Better, Best) is fed as input, and the model learns to produce progressively improved outputs conditioned on that sequence.
teaching_value: Makes the CoH training mechanism concrete by showing how ranked feedback sequences are structured as input, helping students understand how the model learns to self-improve by conditioning on its own history.
when_to_show: Show when teaching how LLMs can be trained to improve outputs using feedback sequences, especially when contrasting CoH with RLHF or other feedback-based training methods.
suggested_caption: CoH fine-tuning: model learns incremental improvement from ranked feedback sequences. (Liu et al., 2023)

description: A diagram illustrating Algorithm Distillation (AD): source policies generate multi-episode learning histories for various tasks, which are then used to train a neural network via behavioral cloning to produce a task-agnostic in-context RL policy.
teaching_value: Clarifies the two-stage process of AD (history generation + behavioral cloning) and shows how in-context reinforcement learning emerges from distilling learning algorithms rather than just solutions.
when_to_show: Show when introducing in-context RL and Algorithm Distillation, particularly to explain how learning histories encode an algorithm rather than just task-specific knowledge.
suggested_caption: Algorithm Distillation: distilling RL learning histories into a neural network. (Laskin et al., 2023)

description: Performance comparison charts of AD, ED (Expert Distillation), source policy, and RL² across memory and exploration environments (dark environments using A3C, watermaze using DQN), measuring cumulative reward over in-context episodes with binary reward.
teaching_value: Empirically demonstrates that AD achieves near-optimal in-context RL performance and shows the importance of multi-episode context length, helping students understand the practical requirements for emergent in-context RL.
when_to_show: Show after explaining AD to validate its performance claims and to discuss why multi-episode context (2–4 episodes) is necessary for near-optimal in-context RL to emerge.
suggested_caption: AD matches or exceeds RL² on memory and exploration tasks. (Laskin et al., 2023)

description: A hierarchical diagram categorizing human memory into sensory memory (iconic, echoic, haptic), short-term/working memory, and long-term memory (explicit/declarative and implicit/procedural), with mappings to LLM agent memory equivalents.
teaching_value: Bridges cognitive science and AI by mapping human memory types to agent memory components (e.g., in-context storage, external vector databases), helping students understand the design rationale for agent memory systems.
when_to_show: Show at the start of the memory module to establish the human memory taxonomy before mapping each type to its LLM agent equivalent.
suggested_caption: Human memory taxonomy mapped to LLM agent memory components. (Weng, 2023)

description: A comparison chart of Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS) / Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) algorithms (e.g., HNSW, FAISS, ScaNN) measured by recall@10 versus query speed, showing the accuracy-speed tradeoff across methods.
teaching_value: Helps students understand the practical tradeoffs when choosing a vector search algorithm for agent memory retrieval, making the abstract concept of ANN concrete with quantitative performance data.
when_to_show: Show when discussing external memory retrieval in agents, specifically to explain why ANN is preferred over exact search and how different algorithms balance speed vs. accuracy.
suggested_caption: MIPS algorithm comparison: recall@10 vs. query speed tradeoffs. (Google Blog, 2020)

description: A multi-stage pipeline diagram showing HuggingGPT's architecture: user request flows through task planning, model selection, task execution, and response generation stages, with the LLM acting as a central controller coordinating specialized AI models from HuggingFace.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how an LLM can orchestrate multiple specialized models as tools, making abstract agent architecture tangible by showing task decomposition, dependency tracking, and multi-model coordination in a single visual flow.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the 'Tool Use' component of LLM agents, specifically to demonstrate how a real system (HuggingGPT) chains task planning → model selection → execution → response, reinforcing the four-stage pipeline described in the text.
suggested_caption: HuggingGPT's four-stage pipeline: LLM plans, selects, and coordinates AI models. (Shen et al. 2023)

description: Pseudo-code block illustrating the decision logic an LLM follows when making API calls in the API-Bank benchmark, showing conditional branching for API selection, parameter filling, and call execution with evaluation checkpoints at each step.
teaching_value: Makes explicit the multi-step decision process hidden inside a single 'tool use' action — students can see that API use involves sequential, evaluable decisions (which API, what parameters, whether to call), not a single atomic step, grounding the benchmark's three-level evaluation framework.
when_to_show: Show after introducing HuggingGPT to deepen understanding of how tool-use accuracy is measured; use it when discussing how researchers evaluate agent capability at granular decision levels rather than just final output quality.
suggested_caption: API-Bank pseudo-code showing LLM decision steps for tool selection and execution. (Li et al. 2023)

description: Architecture diagram of the Generative Agents system showing how a single LLM-powered agent processes observations through a memory stream, retrieves relevant memories via a retrieval module, reflects to form higher-level insights, and plans actions — all feeding back into behavior in a sandbox simulation environment.
teaching_value: Visually connects the memory, reflection, and planning subsystems into a coherent cognitive loop, helping students understand how long-term believable behavior emerges from structured memory retrieval and reflection rather than just reactive prompting.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from tool-use agents to socially-interactive agent simulations; use it to contrast reactive tool-use architectures with agents that require persistent memory and self-reflection to sustain coherent long-term behavior.
suggested_caption: Generative agent architecture: memory stream, retrieval, reflection, and planning loop. (Park et al. 2023)
